text:
---

Les nombres de la nuit écorchent l'air, la lune est à son quatrième quartier, et la Grande Ourse danse une ronde à moitié cachée derrière le voile de l'éther. La nuit a lâché le meneu' de loups, et ses ombres se glissent entre les arbres, murmurant des secrets oubliés aux vents. Les laveuses au gué battent plus fort, leurs gestes se perdent dans l'eau noire, et l'on dirait que les pierres elles-mêmes se mettent à bouger, répondant à l'appel des fades. La Grand'Bête, faite comme un veau et un lièvre, se dresse sur la lisière du bois, son pelage miroitant sous la lueur des flambettes errantes. Les morts reviennent, leurs pas silencieux tracent des chemins que nul vivant ne voit, et l'on sent que la nuit est un pays où tout est possible, où chaque chose a sa vie et son langage. 

---

Ce texte cherche à rendre la voix du scribe plus immersive et mystérieuse, tout en conservant la structure et l'esprit de la description originale. Il vise à évoquer une vision plus onirique et vivante de la nuit, tout en gardant la simplicité et la concision demandées.
text_en: The numbers of the night scrape the air, the moon is at its fourth quarter, and the Great Bear dances a half-hidden round behind the veil of the ether. The night has unleashed the wolf leader, and his shadows slip between the trees, whispering forgotten secrets to the winds. The washerwomen at the ford beat their clothes harder, their motions lost in the dark water, and it seems even the stones themselves begin to move, answering the call of the fades. The Grand Beast, like a calf and a hare, stands at the edge of the wood, its fur shimmering under the light of wandering fires. The dead return, their silent steps tracing paths unseen by the living, and one feels that the night is a land where all is possible, where every thing has its life and its language.
score_en: When the night's quiet is broken by a low, steady hum, like the beat of a washerwoman's wooden paddle on the river's edge, and it grows louder, then softer, then louder again, it's a sign that the night has let loose one of its people. The humming is the sound of the laveuses de nuit, the night washerwomen, who beat their clothes at the river's edge under the light of the moon. They say if you hear this, it's a sign of change, of washing away old troubles, but also of new ones coming. The force of the hum, whether it grows or fades, tells if the change will be gentle or harsh. If it ends with a loud thump, like a paddle striking the water one last time, it's said that the troubles will be strong but short-lived. If it fades away softly, like a whisper, the troubles will be long and quiet, but they will pass. This is how the night speaks, in its own rustic language, through the sounds of its people.
